title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: An environmental organisation has asked you to write a report on the food waste problem in your community. Your report should describe the scale of the problem, identify the main causes, and recommend practical measures that could be taken by individuals, businesses, and local authorities to reduce it.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Report on Food Waste in the Brentford Community

Introduction
This report examines the issue of food waste in the Brentford area, drawing on data from the local council's annual waste audit and a questionnaire completed by 200 local residents and fifteen business owners. It identifies the principal causes of waste and proposes targeted solutions for different stakeholders.

Scale of the Problem
Brentford households collectively discard an estimated 1,200 tonnes of food per year, of which approximately 70% is classed as 'avoidable'—meaning the food was edible at the time of disposal. Local restaurants and cafés contribute a further 300 tonnes annually. At current rates, this represents a significant financial cost for households and a substantial environmental burden in terms of landfill methane emissions.

Main Causes
The key contributing factors are:
- Over-purchasing driven by promotional offers encouraging consumers to buy more than they can use.
- Poor meal planning and a limited understanding of date labelling, with many residents discarding food based on 'best before' rather than 'use by' dates.
- A lack of infrastructure for businesses to donate surplus food to community organisations.

Recommendations
- Individuals: Adopt weekly meal planning and sign up for the council's food waste caddy collection scheme.
- Businesses: Partner with the local food redistribution charity to donate unsold produce at the end of each trading day.
- Local Authority: Launch a public awareness campaign clarifying the legal meaning of 'best before' and 'use by' dates, and introduce mandatory food waste reporting for catering businesses.

Conclusion
Food waste is a solvable problem. A coordinated effort across households, businesses, and the council would produce measurable results within twelve months.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You are a student who has been awarded a scholarship to study abroad for one semester. You have received an offer from a university in a country where you have never lived. Write an email to the international student coordinator at the university introducing yourself, asking for practical information about arriving and settling in, and expressing your enthusiasm for the opportunity.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Ms. Chen,

My name is Marco Ferretti and I am writing to introduce myself as one of the incoming scholarship students joining the Faculty of Economics at your university next semester. I am currently a third-year student at the University of Bologna, and I am genuinely delighted to have been selected for this exchange programme.

As I prepare for my arrival, I have a few practical questions that I hope you will be able to help with. Firstly, I would be grateful to know the process for arranging on-campus accommodation. The scholarship information I received mentioned that housing assistance is available, but I have not yet received instructions on how to apply or what the availability is like for students arriving in September.

I would also appreciate guidance on arriving in the city. Is there a recommended route from the airport to campus, and is there an airport transfer service specifically for international students? Additionally, I understand that I will need to register with a local clinic for healthcare provision—could you point me towards information about how this works for international students?

Finally, I noticed on the university website that there is an international student orientation week before the semester begins. Could you confirm whether attendance is expected and whether I need to register in advance?

I am tremendously excited about this experience and am very much looking forward to meeting you and the other members of the international student community.

With warm regards,
Marco Ferretti

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: A technology magazine is inviting readers to review a mobile application or digital tool that has genuinely improved their daily life. Describe the app or tool, explain its main features, evaluate its effectiveness, and say who would benefit most from using it. Write your review.
tasks.modelAnswer:
A Quiet Revolution in Daily Organisation: Notion

I resisted Notion for longer than I care to admit—another app promising to change how I worked seemed unlikely to deliver on the claim. After eighteen months of daily use, I can report that, on this occasion, the promise was kept.

Notion is a productivity workspace that combines note-taking, project management, database creation, and personal journalling in a single, deeply customisable platform. Unlike conventional note apps, which impose a fixed structure, Notion allows users to design their own systems—from simple to-do lists to elaborate relational databases linking tasks, contacts, projects, and deadlines in genuinely useful ways.

What distinguishes Notion from its many competitors is the quality of the balance it strikes between power and accessibility. Beginners can get useful results immediately using the extensive library of pre-built templates; more experienced users can construct sophisticated systems without requiring any technical knowledge. The interface is clean and satisfying to use, and the ability to embed images, files, and web links directly into notes makes it a genuinely versatile research and writing environment.

My primary criticism is that the mobile application, while functional, is noticeably less fluid than the desktop version, which can be frustrating for on-the-go use. Offline functionality is also limited.

I would recommend Notion particularly to students, writers, freelancers, and anyone managing multiple projects simultaneously. It is not a tool that replaces discipline—no app can—but for those already inclined to organised thinking, it is extraordinarily powerful.
